var-2024-verb2-ELF-039.solution_path: The passage names Snow's 'ultimate concern' as geopolitical: if the democracies don't modernize undeveloped countries, 'the Communist countries will'. The cultural divide mattered to Snow because it kept capable minds away from science, weakening the West's position in the cold war. Answer B.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need the trigger — what especially motivated Snow to write about the two cultures in the first place. Not the surface complaint about literary types; the root reason he thought the gap mattered.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Schism' = a deep split or division. 'Gulf of mutual incomprehension' = the inability of scientists and literary intellectuals to understand each other. 'Geopolitics' = global political competition between nations. Tracking these terms lets you separate proximate complaint from ultimate concern.

steps.title: Locate the cause sentence
steps.text: The writer asks the question directly: 'So why did Snow think the supposed gulf between the two cultures was such a problem?' The answer that follows is the one we want — everything before that is setup.

steps.title: Read the explicit answer
steps.text: The text continues: '…it leads many capable minds to ignore science as a vocation, which prevents us from solving the world's main issue, the wealth gap caused by industrialization, which threatens global stability.' Then it labels this Snow's 'ultimate concern, which has less to do with intellectual life than with geopolitics'.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: 'Urgent political context' in option B is the paraphrase of 'geopolitics' and 'threatens global stability'. The cold-war framing — Communist countries modernizing if the West doesn't — is exactly the urgent political situation Snow had in mind.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: Re-read each wrong option with the geopolitics sentence in hand: A talks about misunderstanding (proximate, not ultimate); C says little role for science (opposite of Snow's view); D is about intellectual quality (a side jab, not the trigger). Only B survives.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer B. The insight in one sentence: when a passage explicitly distinguishes proximate complaints from an 'ultimate concern', the correct option paraphrases the ultimate one.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to take 'gulf of mutual incomprehension' at face value as the main worry.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 shows the passage explicitly labels the geopolitical wealth gap — not the cultural misunderstanding — as Snow's 'ultimate concern'. Misunderstanding is the symptom; geopolitics is the trigger.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'wealth gap caused by industrialization' and infer Snow saw science as marginal to closing it.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 shows the opposite: Snow argued only by erasing the gap between the two cultures — i.e. by deploying more science — could the West ensure wealth and self-government. Science is central, not minor.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on cultural critique is to assume Snow was attacking the humanities' intellectual quality.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 shows Snow's beef is that literary intellectuals ignored science, not that their research was intellectually weak. The complaint is about scope and attitude, not rigor.

var-2024-verb2-ELF-039.technique: On 'cause' questions in dense essays, hunt for the explicit 'why' sentence. The writer often poses the question themselves ('So why did Snow think…?') and then answers it in the next clause. That is the trigger you want — not the upstream complaint.
var-2024-verb2-ELF-039.pitfall: The cure for grabbing the first plausible cause: keep reading until you hit the phrase 'ultimate concern' or 'deeper point' or 'main issue'. The passage flags the answer with such markers; the early complaints are decoys.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the writer asks 'why did X think Y was a problem?', skip the upstream symptoms and lock onto the sentence that follows — that is the cause the question wants.

var-2024-verb2-ELF-040.solution_path: The writer calls the book 'irretrievably a cold war document' yet says its tension about progress 'speaks most directly to us today'. The book is still noteworthy — but for new reasons. Answer C.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need the writer's overall impression of Snow's book. Not Snow's own claims — the reviewer's verdict on whether and why the book still matters in 2024.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Noteworthy' = worth paying attention to. 'Irretrievably' = beyond recovery, permanently. 'Cold war document' = a text that only made sense in the geopolitical context of the 1950s–80s. Read 'noteworthy for other reasons' as 'still worth reading, but the relevance has shifted'.

steps.title: Spot the then-vs-now contrast
steps.text: The reviewer says Snow's expression of optimism is 'dated' but his 'thoughts about progress are more relevant today than his cultural typologies'. That's an explicit then-vs-now contrast: original interest = the cultural split; current interest = the science-progress tension.

steps.title: Confirm with the closing
steps.text: The final paragraph says the book's 'deepest tensions in our ideas about progress' is 'the aspect of Snow's book that speaks most directly to us today'. And it concludes 'we should spend less time merely citing The Two Cultures, and more time genuinely reconsidering it' — i.e. still worth reading, but rethink what you read it for.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option C — 'noteworthy for other reasons than in the mid-20th century' — is a clean paraphrase of 'progress thoughts now matter more than the cultural typologies'. The 'other reasons' captures the shift in what makes it interesting.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A claims the writer can't grasp the original controversy — but the writer cites Leavis and Trilling, showing he understands it. B says the political-moral message retains its force — contradicted by 'irretrievably a cold war document'. D claims the two-cultures split is still valid — contradicted by 'a very slippery thing' and 'less time merely citing'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer C. The insight in one sentence: when a review draws a then-vs-now line ('dated… more relevant today'), the right answer captures the reason-shift rather than continued original force.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the reviewer's measured tone as evidence he cannot see what the original fuss was about.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the reviewer cites the Leavis and Trilling responses directly — he understands the controversy fully, he just thinks newer readers should focus on a different aspect of the book.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the heavy political content and infer the political-moral message is still in force.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 shows the reviewer calls the book 'irretrievably a cold war document' and 'dated' — the original political force is precisely what has been lost.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a 60-year-old cultural critique is that the split it identified still holds.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 shows the reviewer calls 'the separateness of Snow's two cultures' a 'very slippery thing' and tells readers to stop 'merely citing' it — the split-as-described isn't his idea of what still works.

var-2024-verb2-ELF-040.technique: On 'writer's main impression' questions about old books, look for the then-vs-now seam: phrases like 'dated', 'more relevant today', 'irretrievably', 'speaks most directly to us now'. The right answer almost always names the relevance shift, not the original message.
var-2024-verb2-ELF-040.pitfall: The cure for grabbing 'still relevant' answers: track WHAT is still relevant. If only one strand survives ('thoughts about progress') and others are explicitly retired ('cultural typologies are dated'), the answer must say 'noteworthy for OTHER reasons' — not 'still in force'.

pregrade_tactic.move: Find the sentence where the reviewer says one strand is 'dated' while another 'speaks today' — the answer is the strand-shift, not continuity.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The passage lists comics' inherent strengths but then pivots: 'making good comics takes as much time and skill as making a good documentary' and 'the communication of science in comics will be only as good as the comics themselves'. Effectiveness depends on design quality. Answer B.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need the writer's central argument about comics for science learning. Not just one feature — the conclusion they reach about what makes comics actually work.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Promising tool' = potentially useful but not yet proven. 'A more fundamental question' = the deeper test the writer wants to apply. When an author flags a 'fundamental question' or a 'however', the argument's centre of gravity is downstream of that pivot.

steps.title: Track the pivot
steps.text: Paragraph one praises comics: storytelling + diagrams + metaphor. Then comes 'A more fundamental question, however, remains to be answered: Do comics actually improve learning or engagement with science?' That 'however' signals the writer is moving from features to test.

steps.title: Read the verdict
steps.text: The writer concedes some research is encouraging but then warns: 'although they may be easy to read, making good comics takes as much time and skill as making a good documentary. At the end of the day, the communication of science in comics will be only as good as the comics themselves.' The verdict is conditional on craft.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option B — 'Making them effective requires them to be very well designed' — paraphrases 'as much time and skill as a good documentary' + 'only as good as the comics themselves'. Effectiveness hinges on design quality.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A says comics mainly simplify — but the passage credits comics with story + structure + metaphor, not simplification specifically. C predicts entertainment-only — the passage frames comics as a learning tool. D says they target the already-interested — contradicted by 'engaging those readers who are less likely to seek other forms of science communication'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer B. The insight in one sentence: when a passage names a tool's promise and then attaches a 'but only if…' clause, the right answer captures the condition, not the promise.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'comics' and assume the writer's point is that they simplify science.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 shows the writer credits comics with combining storytelling, diagram structure, and metaphor — not bare simplification. The argument is about craft, not dumbing down.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'engaging storytelling' and predict comics will drift toward entertainment.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 shows the writer treats comics as a science-communication tool throughout — the question is whether they educate, not whether they entertain. The 'entertainment-only future' is not the argument.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on niche media is that they preach to the converted.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage explicitly says comics may engage 'readers who are less likely to seek other forms of science communication' — they target NEW audiences, not just current science fans.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-031.technique: On 'what is argued' questions, find the conditional sentence — usually a 'however', 'yet', or 'only as good as' clause near the end of the passage. The writer's actual argument lives in that condition, not in the upstream list of features.
var-2026-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: The cure for picking 'feature' answers: ask 'what does the writer say has to be TRUE for this to work?' Features describe what comics could do; the argument is the condition that decides whether they actually will.

pregrade_tactic.move: Find the 'only as good as…' or 'requires…' clause in the closing — the right answer paraphrases the condition, not the upstream promise.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: Olivola and Todorov's conclusion: appearance-based judgments are 'particularly hard to correct' because they are 'speedy, automatic, and implicit', and 'often people don't even recognize they are forming judgments about others from their appearances'. Unconscious opinions resist change. Answer D.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: 'What is said here?' — i.e. what claim is the passage making? We're looking for the option that the text actually supports, not the option that sounds true in general.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Implicit' = below the level of conscious awareness. 'Trait inference' = guess about someone's personality. 'Unawares' / 'unconscious' = without the person noticing. These are technical terms the passage hangs its argument on.

steps.title: Locate the conclusion sentence
steps.text: Olivola and Todorov say plainly: 'Getting people to overcome the influence of first impressions will not be an easy task. The speed, automaticity, and implicit nature of appearance-based trait inferences make them particularly hard to correct.' That's the explicit claim.

steps.title: Read the second part
steps.text: And: 'Moreover, often people don't even recognize that they are forming judgments about others from their appearances.' That extends the point — unconscious formation makes correction harder.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option D — 'Opinions formed unawares are often more difficult to change than conscious ones' — paraphrases 'implicit… particularly hard to correct' + 'don't even recognize they are forming judgments'. Direct hit on the conclusion.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A invents an overconfident-vs-clever-looking comparison the passage never makes. B says the brain is good at distinguishing inherent traits — but the passage describes voters being SWAYED by appearance, not being accurate. C says elections are decided ENTIRELY by appearance — the passage says voters 'rely heavily', not exclusively.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer D. The insight in one sentence: 'what is said here' questions reward the option that paraphrases an actual sentence — not the option that sounds like a reasonable extension.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'rapid judgments about personality' and import a familiar finding about confident-looking candidates.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage never compares overconfident-looking to clever-looking candidates. Bringing in outside knowledge is exactly the trap on 'what is said here' questions.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'predict electoral success' and infer the brain is accurate at reading inherent traits.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage describes voters being swayed by appearance — it does not claim those appearance-based inferences are accurate. Predicting election outcomes is about what voters DO, not what is TRUE.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on appearance-driven voting is to maximize: elections are decided entirely by looks.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage says voters 'rely heavily' on appearance and navigate 'a flood of information' — appearance is a major shortcut, but not the only input.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-032.technique: On 'what is said here?' questions, find the option that quotes — almost verbatim — a sentence from the passage. Reject options that generalise, maximise ('entirely'), or import outside knowledge.
var-2026-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: The cure for over-reading: anchor every wrong option against a specific sentence. If the option's claim doesn't appear anywhere in the passage, no matter how plausible, it's not 'said here'.

pregrade_tactic.move: Pick the option that is the tightest paraphrase of an actual sentence — reject anything that says 'entirely', 'never' or imports beyond-text knowledge.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The first two paragraphs set up the romantic 'bygone era' image and then deliver an 'unexpected plot twist – independent bookstores are actually really healthy', backed by seven straight years of growth. The death story was exaggerated. Answer D.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need the basic message of paragraphs 1 and 2 — the headline a reader would take away after the first 200 words.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Moribund' = nearly dead. 'Plot twist' = an unexpected reversal. 'Greatly exaggerated' alludes to Mark Twain's joke ('reports of my death have been greatly exaggerated') — a common idiom for 'they're not as dead as you thought'.

steps.title: Trace the structure of paragraph 1
steps.text: Paragraph 1 paints bookstores as romantic, moribund, 'last chance to see' — the standard story. Then it flips: 'Ironically, that reputation may have contributed to an unexpected plot twist – independent bookstores are actually really healthy.' The pivot is the punchline.

steps.title: Confirm with the numbers in paragraph 2
steps.text: Paragraph 2 backs the twist with data: ABA membership grew from 1,712 to 1,775 in one year, seven straight years of growth, sales up ~5% in early 2016, share up from 7% to 10%. The death narrative is clearly contradicted.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option D — 'The death of the bookstore seems to have been greatly exaggerated' — is the exact idiomatic match for 'unexpected plot twist + seven straight years of growth'. The Twain echo is doing the work.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A says demand has been in gradual decline — contradicted by 'seven straight years' growth. B says the emotional image can be retired — the passage uses the romance, doesn't reject it. C says paper is dying — the passage celebrates indies and (later) paper books holding their own.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer D. The insight in one sentence: when a passage opens with a 'reports-of-death' twist, the right answer almost always names the exaggeration.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'bygone era' and 'moribund' as evidence of long, gradual decline.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 shows the numbers refute decline: seven straight years of growth and rising sales. The 'bygone era' framing is precisely what the passage flips.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the romantic-feelings opening and assume the writer is dismissing that image as outdated.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 shows the passage uses the romantic image as setup, not as a target. The pivot isn't 'stop being sentimental' — it's 'these stores aren't actually dying'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a piece about bookstore survival is to frame it against the death of paper books.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the first two paragraphs say nothing about paper-vs-digital — that argument arrives later in the passage. The opening is about bookstores, not formats.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-033.technique: On 'message of the first two paragraphs' questions, locate the pivot sentence ('Ironically', 'But', 'an unexpected twist') — the message is whatever comes AFTER the pivot, not the setup before it.
var-2026-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: The cure for choosing the setup: ignore the romantic/dystopian opening framing. The writer is telling you they're going to overturn it. The right answer is the overturning, not the thing being overturned.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the opening builds a 'dying' image then pivots with 'ironically' or 'plot twist', pick the option that names the exaggeration of the original obituary.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The passage contrasts the US (seven straight years of indie growth) with the UK ('the number of independent bookstores shrank 3% in 2015'). Prospects vary by country. Answer C.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need what the passage argues about independent bookstores — specifically the geographic and market picture, not their cultural appeal.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Prospects' = outlook, future trajectory. 'Vary' = differ. When an option says 'vary a great deal between countries', look in the passage for explicit country contrasts.

steps.title: Find the US-UK contrast
steps.text: The passage says US indies are growing (1,712 → 1,775 stores, seventh straight year) and 'indies accounted for about 10% of all books sold in 2015, up from 7%'. Then: 'It's also not true in the U.K., where the number of independent bookstores (sorry, bookshops) shrank 3% in 2015.'

steps.title: Notice the contrast marker
steps.text: 'It's also not true in the U.K.' is the explicit signal that the writer is drawing a country contrast. The growth story is US-specific; the UK has the opposite trend. Two countries, opposite directions.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option C — 'Their prospects seem to vary a great deal between countries' — is the direct paraphrase of US-growing-vs-UK-shrinking. The 'vary' lands cleanly on the US/UK split.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A says they have a 'dominant market share' — 10% is notable but not dominant. B predicts 'a brilliant future on a global scale' — the UK shrinkage rules out global. D says 'still a long way from realizing their full market potential' — the passage doesn't speak to untapped market; it celebrates current growth where it exists.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer C. The insight in one sentence: when the passage offers a 'true in X, not true in Y' contrast, the right answer captures the variation, not a global generalisation in either direction.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'seven straight years' of growth and 10% share as a dominant position.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows 10% is a notable but minority share — chain stores and online retailers still hold the larger pieces. 'Dominant' overstates what the numbers support.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the upbeat US numbers and project a 'brilliant global future' for indies.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 shows the UK 3% shrinkage is the explicit counterexample. A global-brilliant story collapses the moment you account for the UK contrast.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a small-share-but-growing story is to say there's untapped market potential ahead.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage doesn't argue indies have unrealised market potential — it argues they have a clear (and modest) niche in the US and are shrinking in the UK. Potential isn't the frame.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-034.technique: On 'what is argued in relation to X' questions, hunt for the explicit contrast marker ('also not true in', 'unlike in', 'by contrast'). The right answer almost always paraphrases the contrast itself rather than picking one side as universal.
var-2026-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: The cure for projecting US growth globally: read the next paragraph. The writer almost always offsets one country's story with another — when they do, 'vary by country' is the answer, not 'brilliant future everywhere'.

pregrade_tactic.move: When a passage cites growth data and then says 'it's also not true in [other country]', pick the option that names cross-country variation, not the cheerleader option.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: Brian Lampkin, the Scuppernong owner, says 'We're letting Amazon and Barnes & Noble take care of the best sellers. Where are you going to get poetry? Some Barnes & Nobles you walk into, you're lucky to find Emily Dickinson.' Indies fill a niche — poetry and specialised literature — that chains and online retailers ignore. Answer A.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need what is implied about the indie comeback — the deeper reason indies are doing well, not just the surface tactics.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Implied' = suggested but not stated outright. 'Limited readership' = niche audience, smaller than mainstream. When an option says 'fill a gap', look in the passage for things big retailers DON'T do.

steps.title: Read the Lampkin quote
steps.text: Lampkin says Scuppernong stocks a 'literary-leaning list' and adds: 'We're letting Amazon and Barnes & Noble take care of the best sellers. Where are you going to get poetry? Some Barnes & Nobles you walk into, you're lucky to find Emily Dickinson.' Indies cede the mainstream and pick up the niche.

steps.title: Connect to discovery research
steps.text: Right after Lampkin, the passage cites consumer research: 'the best method for book discovery is still standing in a roomful of books and browsing — ahead even of click-tracking, data-mining algorithms.' Indies enable browsing in a curated literary space — exactly the niche-discovery service.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option A — 'They fill a gap with regard to literature aimed at a more limited readership' — is the direct implication of the poetry/literary-leaning quote. Niche literature = limited readership.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: B says e-books threaten indies — but the passage describes e-books receding (28% → 24%). C says social media drives indie profits — social media is one of several mundane causes, not the centre. D predicts online retailers will kill indies — but the passage says both are growing.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer A. The insight in one sentence: when an indie's owner says 'we let the chains have the best sellers, we cover what they don't', the right answer is the niche-filling story.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to assume e-books are the natural enemy of any bookstore.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage actually frames e-books as receding (from 28% to 24%), and indie growth coincides with that retreat. E-books aren't threatening indies in this story.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the social-media mention among the causes and infer it as the main revenue driver.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows social media is listed alongside accounting tools and Borders' collapse as 'prosaic' reasons. The deeper, less-tangible reason is the niche fit Lampkin describes.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on online competition is that bricks-and-mortar will eventually lose.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage explicitly notes online retailers booming AND indies gaining ground in parallel — not a zero-sum fight. Even Amazon opened physical stores.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-035.technique: On 'what is implied' questions about why a niche player is winning, hunt for a quote where the niche player explicitly cedes the mainstream ('we let X take care of Y') and names what they cover instead. That quoted division of labour is the implication.
var-2026-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: The cure for picking 'social media' or 'e-book threat' answers: separate the prosaic causes (technology, social media, Borders' collapse) from the deeper reason. The question asks about implication — that's the deeper, less-tangible reason, not the bullet-list mundane ones.

pregrade_tactic.move: Find the quote where the owner says 'we let the big guys take care of X' — the answer is the gap they fill, not the cause-list earlier in the paragraph.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: The passage tracks e-books from 9% of unit sales in 2010 to 28% in 2013, when 'eventual dominance began to feel like technological manifest destiny'. Then in 2015 'the share of e-books actually receded to 24%' — an unexpected setback to the conquest story. Answer D.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need the passage's claim about e-books — specifically how their trajectory has played out, not whether they're good or bad for reading.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Setback' = an unexpected reverse after progress. 'Manifest destiny' = a sense of inevitable expansion (from US 19th-century rhetoric). 'Receded' = pulled back. The combination 'unexpected setback' fits 'rose then fell back' tightly.

steps.title: Trace the numbers
steps.text: 9% (2010) → 28% (2013) → 24% (2015). The 28% peak was when dominance 'began to feel like technological manifest destiny'. The 24% is a real, measurable retreat — not the trajectory anyone predicted.

steps.title: Note the writer's framing
steps.text: The writer prefaces the data with 'a surprise twist in the story of the e-book' — i.e. an unexpected development. That phrase is the structural marker that the answer involves surprise plus reversal.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option D — 'Their continuing success story has suffered an unexpected setback' — pairs 'success story' (9% → 28%) with 'unexpected setback' (28% → 24%). It mirrors both the rise and the surprise reversal.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A says e-books were never seen as a serious threat — contradicted by 'began to feel like technological manifest destiny'. B says takeover is unstoppable — contradicted by the 24% retreat. C says e-books will stay marginal for serious literature — 24% is far from marginal, and the passage doesn't carve out literary readers.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer D. The insight in one sentence: when a passage gives three data points showing rise-then-fall and calls the fall a 'surprise twist', the answer pairs the trajectory with 'unexpected setback'.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the e-book retreat as proof e-books were never really threatening.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage explicitly says e-book dominance 'began to feel like technological manifest destiny' — the threat was real and widely felt at the 28% peak.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the early growth curve (9% → 28%) and project unstoppable takeover.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 shows the trajectory actually reversed: the 24% in 2015 is lower than the 28% in 2013. Unstoppable takeovers don't go backwards.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a setback is that e-books are being rejected for serious literature.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows 24% market share is substantial, not marginal. And the passage doesn't draw a 'serious literature vs popular' line — it tracks the overall non-self-published market.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-036.technique: On 'what is said as regards X' questions involving numbers, lay the data points end to end. If they describe rise-then-fall and the writer flags the fall as 'surprise' or 'unexpected', the answer is 'setback to the success story', not 'never a threat' or 'inevitable triumph'.
var-2026-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: The cure for over-correcting either direction: 24% isn't 0% and 28% wasn't 100%. Pick the option that honours both the rise and the reversal — not the option that erases one of them.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the numbers go up then down and the writer says 'surprise twist', pick the option that says 'success story + unexpected setback' — not the option that nukes either end of the arc.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The closing says indies face challenges, 'theirs is not a huge growth business. No one's getting rich' — and Lampkin notes 'there's no real reason to do this other than love and commitment'. The motive is non-financial. Answer B.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need the passage's concluding take on indie bookstores — not the early data on growth, but the final note the writer wants to leave the reader with.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'In simplistic terms' = boiled down to its essence. 'Not in it just for the money' = motivated by something other than profit. When an owner quote names 'love and commitment' as the reason, look for an option that captures non-financial motivation.

steps.title: Read the closing paragraph
steps.text: 'No question, indie bookstores face challenges. Theirs is not a huge growth business. No one's getting rich.' Then Lampkin: 'I think they sort of get that there's no real reason to do this other than love and commitment.' The closing pivots from market data to motive.

steps.title: Compare to option language
steps.text: Option B says 'In simplistic terms, they do not seem to be in it just for the money.' 'Simplistic terms' echoes the punchy closing tone ('No one's getting rich'). 'Not in it just for the money' is the exact paraphrase of 'no real reason to do this other than love and commitment'.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option B captures both the closing data ('no one's getting rich') and the closing quote (love/commitment as the motive). It's the only option that names motive rather than predicting market outcomes.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A says long-term profitability is beyond doubt — contradicted by 'no one's getting rich'. C says they'll be temporary — the passage notes seven years of growth and ongoing gain. D says lucrative business — contradicted by Lampkin's 'no real reason… other than love and commitment'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer B. The insight in one sentence: when a passage ends with a market dataset and an owner quote about motive, the conclusion is usually about motive — not extrapolated profitability.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to take 'gaining ground' as evidence of a long-term profitable strategy.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the closing says explicitly 'no one's getting rich' — long-term profitability isn't being claimed, let alone 'beyond doubt'.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the closing notes on Amazon's brick-and-mortar push and predict indies will be eclipsed.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage celebrates seven straight years of indie growth and frames their survival as the story — not a temporary blip.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on rising sales and store counts is that the business is becoming lucrative.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 shows the closing flatly contradicts this: 'No one's getting rich' and 'love and commitment' as the reason. Growth without lucrativeness is exactly the writer's point.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-037.technique: On 'what is concluded at the end' questions, locate the final owner quote — it often distils the writer's verdict. If the quote names motivation rather than market outcome, the answer is the motivation paraphrase, not the projected business prospect.
var-2026-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: The cure for confusing growth with profit: track the two separately. The passage shows growth ('seventh straight year', 5% sales up) AND modest economics ('no one's getting rich'). The right closing answer holds both — growth happens, but profits don't drive the story.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the final owner quote names motive ('love', 'commitment', 'thank you') rather than dollars, pick the option that says 'not in it just for the money'.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The passage argues that as recipes get reinscribed across generations and cultures, 'each new layer tells us something about a different group of consumers: their tastes, their economy, their language and their relationship with those from whom they adopted the dish'. Modifications reveal the eaters. Answer D.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need the passage's main argument — the thesis it builds toward, not the surface observation that recipes change.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Reinscribed' = rewritten / re-marked. 'Accretion' = a slow build-up of layers. 'Indirect clues' = signals you read off the artefact rather than being told directly. When the passage talks about 'each new layer tells us something', think 'modifications = evidence about the modifiers'.

steps.title: Locate the thesis sentence
steps.text: 'But while the accretion of such alterations may obscure the original, each new "layer" tells us something about a different group of consumers: their tastes, their economy, their language and their relationship with those from whom they adopted the dish.' That sentence is the writer's thesis.

steps.title: Note the diagnostic function
steps.text: The thesis treats recipe modifications as DATA about the people who do the modifying. Their tastes, economy, language, and intercultural relationships all leave traces in the new version of the dish. The dish, in other words, indirectly reveals its eaters.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option D — 'Modified dishes may give indirect clues about the people who eat them' — is a direct paraphrase: 'each new layer tells us something about a different group of consumers' = 'indirect clues about the people who eat them'.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A says recipe changes appeal to new consumers — the passage isn't about marketing strategy. B says taste evolution causes acceptance of foreign dishes — the passage tracks what changes reveal, not how acceptance grows. C says modified dishes are only loosely tied to originals — partially true but it's a side observation, not the main argument.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer D. The insight in one sentence: when a passage frames an artefact's changes as evidence about its users, the main argument is the diagnostic claim — not the appeal claim or the drift-from-original claim.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'passed between cultures' as a marketing story about appealing to new consumers.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage doesn't argue recipe changes are designed to appeal — it argues recipe changes REVEAL who is adapting them. The frame is diagnostic, not commercial.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'passed between cultures' and infer the argument is about how foreign food becomes accepted.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 shows the thesis sentence is about what modifications REVEAL — not how new tastes drive acceptance. Acceptance is upstream of the writer's point.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on accumulated changes is to focus on how far the new version drifts from the original.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows that 'obscure the original' is mentioned in passing — the writer immediately pivots to what the layers TELL US about consumers. Drift is acknowledged; it's not the main argument.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-038.technique: On 'main argument' questions, find the sentence where the writer says 'X tells us about Y'. That diagnostic move is almost always the thesis — adaptation as evidence about the adapters.
var-2026-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: The cure for picking the surface observation: ask 'what does the writer want me to LEARN from these changes?' If the answer is 'something about the eaters', pick the option that names that diagnostic reading.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage says modifications 'tell us something about' a group, pick the option that says modifications give indirect clues — not the option about appeal, evolution, or drift.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The passage says when an albatross female deems a partnership unsuccessful, 'the process is relatively understated and free from noisy squabbles', and 'she will simply appear with a different male in the following breeding season'. The implicit contrast is with human breakups, which the passage frames via 'divorce' and 'romantic-seeming marriages'. Less dramatic than humans. Answer A.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need what is IMPLIED about albatross couples — what the passage suggests without saying outright, especially through the comparison with humans.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Understated' = quiet, low-key. 'Free from noisy squabbles' = without loud fights. 'Mate for life' = lifelong partnership. The passage uses human words for animal behaviour — 'marriage', 'divorce', 'partnership' — which sets up an implicit comparison with humans.

steps.title: Locate the separation description
steps.text: 'The process is relatively understated and free from noisy squabbles, says University of Lisbon biologist Francesco Ventura. Often when a female deems the partnership unsuccessful over the course of a year, she will simply appear with a different male in the following breeding season.' Quiet swap, not high drama.

steps.title: Read the implicit human comparison
steps.text: By using 'divorce' and 'marriage' in scare quotes for albatrosses, the writer invites the reader to compare with human breakups — which famously involve squabbles, drama, court fights. The contrast is the implication.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option A — 'They separate in a less dramatic fashion than humans do' — is the precise implication: understated + no noisy squabbles + simply appearing with a new partner = less dramatic than the human equivalent.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: B says less stable than most birds — passage says they 'often mate for life', i.e. quite stable. C says behaviour not yet understood — the passage gives a clear account from a named biologist. D says partnerships more temporary than humans' — opposite: 'often mate for life' is typically longer than human marriages.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer A. The insight in one sentence: when a passage uses human words (marriage, divorce) for animal behaviour and then describes the animal version as 'understated', the implication is 'less dramatic than the human version'.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'divorce is not unheard of' as evidence partnerships are less stable than other birds'.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage explicitly says albatrosses 'often mate for life' — they're highly stable as bird partnerships go. Divorce being possible isn't the same as instability being the norm.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'a female will simply appear with a different male' and infer the underlying mechanism isn't yet understood.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage gives a clear behavioural account from a named biologist (Ventura) with a plausible reason (lack of breeding success). The behaviour is well-described, not mysterious.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on bird relationships is to assume short attachment compared to humans.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage says albatrosses 'mate for life' — that's typically LONGER than current human marriage averages, not shorter. The temporal comparison goes the opposite way.

var-2026-verb1-ELF-039.technique: On 'what is implied' questions about animal behaviour described in human vocabulary ('marriage', 'divorce'), the implication is usually a CONTRAST: the animal version is quieter, simpler, or more practical than the human equivalent the writer is invoking.
var-2026-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: The cure for inverting the comparison: re-read the description. If the animal behaviour is described as 'understated' or 'free from noisy squabbles', the implied contrast is with the loud, dramatic human equivalent — not in the other direction.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage uses human terms ('marriage', 'divorce') for animals and adds 'understated' or 'quiet', pick the option that says the animal version is less dramatic than the human one.

var-2026-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The passage contrasts US owners (70% indoor cats, motivated by wildlife and traffic concerns) with UK and European owners (70% outdoor, justified by 'cats' wellbeing'). The cats-on-wildlife conversation is well-developed in the US but has had limited reach in Britain. Answer B.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need what can be concluded from the first two paragraphs — the takeaway from the US-vs-UK setup, not the later debate about specific solutions.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Limited attention' = the topic has been discussed but not much. 'Laissez-faire' = a hands-off attitude. The US has shifted from 35% indoor cats in the 1990s to 70% today; the UK is still at 70% outdoor — that's where the asymmetry in attention lies.

steps.title: Trace the US vs UK contrast
steps.text: Paragraph 1: US debate 'raging for years'; research shows cats kill billions; 70% of US owners keep cats indoors. Paragraph 2: UK owners 'feel differently'; 70% let cats out; 'cats' wellbeing' is the framing; the EU even endorses free movement. McDonald: 'massive societal difference [from the US].'

steps.title: Identify the implied claim
steps.text: The first two paragraphs show one country (US) actively wrestling with cats-killing-wildlife and another (UK + Europe) prioritising cat wellbeing instead. The implicit conclusion: in Britain, the wildlife-harm angle has had relatively little airtime — not zero, but limited.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option B — 'The negative impact of cats has so far received limited attention in Britain' — paraphrases the US-engaged / UK-cat-wellbeing-first framing. Limited attention is exactly the asymmetry described.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A says European cats are 'overprotected' — the passage describes them being LET OUT, not over-protected. C says Europeans 'deliberately ignored' risks — the passage doesn't say deliberate, just that wellbeing is the priority. D says Americans exaggerate figures — the passage cites US research approvingly, not skeptically.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer B. The insight in one sentence: when a passage shows two countries with opposite priorities, the right takeaway is the relative attention gap in the country where the topic gets less focus — not a moral judgement on either side.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'cats' wellbeing' framing as evidence European cats are over-protected.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the European framing emphasises FREEDOM (free movement, outdoor access) — the opposite of over-protection. Cats roam more, not less.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'feel differently' and infer European owners are deliberately dismissing the wildlife risks.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage frames the difference as cultural priority, not conscious dismissal. The wildlife risks haven't been the central concern — but that's distance, not active ignoring.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a cross-country contrast is to suspect the more alarming figures are inflated.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage cites US research uncritically and later applies the same logic to the UK ('UK cats kill 160 to 270 million animals annually'). The numbers aren't being challenged.

var-2026-verb2-ELF-036.technique: On 'conclusion from the first two paragraphs' questions, find the explicit cross-country contrast and ask which side has been engaging less with the topic. The right answer names the attention gap, not the moral character of either side.
var-2026-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: The cure for inferring deliberateness: read the framing words. 'Feel differently' and 'considered good for cats' wellbeing' are cultural-priority phrases, not 'consciously ignoring risk' phrases. Limited attention is the right diagnosis.

pregrade_tactic.move: When two countries have opposite practices (70% indoor vs 70% outdoor) for opposite reasons, pick the option that names the under-discussed angle in the second country — not deliberate dismissal.

var-2026-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The passage states 'cats are prolific hunters of wildlife in the UK and Europe, too', cites UK cats killing 160–270 million animals annually, and notes the real figure may be higher. The 'too' explicitly equates UK/European cats with American ones as predators. Answer C.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need what the passage says about UK and European cats specifically — their hunting impact compared with American cats.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Prolific hunters' = kill a lot of prey. The word 'too' is doing critical work: 'cats are prolific hunters in the UK and Europe, too' presupposes the same is already true elsewhere — and joins the UK side to the American side as equally dangerous.

steps.title: Locate the equating sentence
steps.text: 'But cats are prolific hunters of wildlife in the UK and Europe, too. A study based on the 2011 pet population estimates that UK cats kill 160 to 270 million animals annually, a quarter of them birds. The real figure is likely to be even higher.' That's the direct claim.

steps.title: Distinguish cats from wildlife adaptation
steps.text: Later, the passage says Europeans have 'a much longer history of dealing with' cats — but that's about wildlife adapting, not about cats becoming less dangerous. The cats themselves still hunt at the same scale; the ecosystems have had longer to absorb the pressure.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option C — 'They are no less dangerous to other animals than are American cats' — paraphrases 'prolific hunters… too'. 'No less dangerous' is the negative-construction equivalent of 'equally dangerous'.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A says they cause less harm than American cats — contradicted by the 'too' and the 160–270 million figure. B says hunting behaviour modified by long history — the passage attributes the adaptation to wildlife, not cat behaviour. D says owners domesticated cats differently — the passage describes the same species, with variation only in owner practice (indoor vs outdoor), not in domestication.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer C. The insight in one sentence: the word 'too' in 'prolific hunters… too' is a tight equating signal — the answer says UK cats are 'no less dangerous' than American ones.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'much longer history of dealing with any impact' as evidence cats cause less harm in Europe.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the 160–270 million annual kills (and 'likely even higher') match or exceed the American framing. The 'history' point is about wildlife adapting, not cats hunting less.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the long European history and infer cats have evolved more gentle hunting behaviour.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 shows the adaptation belongs to the WILDLIFE, not the cats. Cats are still 'prolific hunters' in the UK — their behaviour is not modified, only the ecosystem's tolerance is.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on cross-country cat behaviour differences is to credit different domestication.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage talks about owner practice (indoor vs outdoor), not about cats themselves being domesticated differently. The species is the same; the management differs.

var-2026-verb2-ELF-037.technique: On 'what is said about X' questions involving cross-country comparisons, look for the equating word — 'too', 'as well', 'no less than'. These words signal that the answer flattens the apparent geographic difference, not amplifies it.
var-2026-verb2-ELF-037.pitfall: The cure for confusing wildlife adaptation with cat adaptation: separate the actor. If the passage says 'we've had longer to deal with the impact', the WILDLIFE has had longer — not the cats. Cats remain equally lethal predators.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage says 'cats are prolific hunters in X, too', pick the option that says UK cats are no less dangerous — not the option that credits long coexistence to softer cats.

var-2026-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: The RSPB 'is not particularly concerned about the impact of cats on the British mainland' and points to global warming, intensive agriculture, and urban expansion as the actual drivers. Cats mostly take 'the doomed surplus' — weak or injured birds. Cats aren't a significant role in bird decline. Answer C.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need the conclusion about cats specifically as a CAUSE of bird decline in Britain — not just whether cats kill birds, but whether they meaningfully drive population decline.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Significant role' = a meaningful contribution to the trend. 'Doomed surplus' = ecology term for individuals likely to die anyway from non-predator causes. If predators take from the doomed surplus, they don't lower the population — they just consume what was already lost.

steps.title: Read the RSPB position
steps.text: 'The UK's biggest bird charity, the Royal Society for the Protection of Birds, is not particularly concerned about the impact of cats on the British mainland. Instead, it focuses on what it says is driving UK bird declines: global warming, intensive agriculture and expanding towns and cities leading to habitat and food loss.' That's the central UK authority's view.

steps.title: Add the doomed-surplus argument
steps.text: 'A big reason why it is less worried is the evidence that cats primarily take "the doomed surplus": weak or injured birds likely to die anyway.' This is the mechanism that exonerates cats from driving decline — they don't subtract additional birds beyond the natural baseline.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option C — 'Overall, cats can hardly be assigned a significant role' — paraphrases 'not particularly concerned' + 'doomed surplus' + 'climate/agriculture/urbanisation are the drivers'. Cats aren't the meaningful actor in the decline story.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A says cats have been universally blamed — the RSPB and Baker explicitly don't blame them. B says cat owners fail their responsibility — the question is about cats' role in DECLINE, not about owner duty. D says cats' impact on bird habitats is beyond doubt — habitat loss is attributed to climate/agriculture/urbanisation, not cats.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer C. The insight in one sentence: when the leading bird-protection authority isn't worried about predator X and names other causes for the decline, the right answer downgrades X's role.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the cats-kill-millions framing as 'universally blamed' for bird decline.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows both the RSPB and Baker explicitly DON'T blame cats — they name climate, agriculture, urbanisation. Universal blame is the opposite of the experts' position.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the welfare-issue framing and infer the answer is about owner responsibility.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the question is about cats' role in bird DECLINE, not about whether owners are responsible. Owner duty is a separate axis the passage doesn't pin to bird-decline causality.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on cats and birds is that cats clearly harm bird habitats.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3 shows habitat loss is attributed to human activity (climate, agriculture, urbanisation). Cats are predators, not habitat destroyers — the harm pathway in option D is misassigned.

var-2026-verb2-ELF-038.technique: On 'conclusion about cats and decline' questions, look for the named drivers of the decline. If the passage lists climate, agriculture, urbanisation, and the predator authority isn't worried, the predator gets a small role — not the headline blame.
var-2026-verb2-ELF-038.pitfall: The cure for conflating 'cats kill birds' with 'cats drive population decline': those are different claims. Cats can kill many individuals without lowering the population if they take the doomed surplus. That distinction is the whole answer.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage cites 'doomed surplus' and names climate/agriculture as decline drivers, pick the option that says cats can hardly be assigned a significant role — not the option that blames cats universally.

var-2026-verb2-ELF-039.solution_path: The passage describes 'a growing minority' of British cat owners keeping cats fully indoors and Cats Protection recommending a 'dusk-till-dawn cat curfew'. Even the conflicted experts (Pirie, Cummings) discuss buffer zones and curfews. The trend is toward LESS outdoor freedom. Answer A.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need what the passage tells us about the current British debate on cats — the direction it's moving, not the static state of practice.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Trend' = the direction of change. 'Curfew' = a time-limited restriction on movement. 'Growing minority' = still a minority but increasing. When the article describes a movement away from current practice, the trend points away from the status quo.

steps.title: Read the direction signals
steps.text: 'Though it is early days, a growing minority are keeping their cats fully indoors.' Cats Protection 'recommends a dusk-till-dawn cat curfew'. Pirie suggests 'cat-free buffer zones around conservation areas'. All three signals point AWAY from unrestricted outdoor access.

steps.title: Note the counter-current
steps.text: Cummings warns that if indoor-only became the norm, some cats would have 'significantly reduced welfare' — there's pushback. But the direction of motion is still toward more restriction; the pushback is a brake, not a reversal.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option A — 'The current trend seems to be heading towards less freedom for cats to spend time outdoors' — paraphrases 'growing minority indoors' + 'curfew' + 'buffer zones'. The qualifier 'seems' fits 'it is early days'.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: B says little interest in the basic issues — but the passage describes active debate with multiple named experts. C says most cat-owners are worried — passage says 'a growing minority', not most. D says cat lovers are 'dead against' restriction — Cummings recommends a curfew, Pirie suggests buffer zones; they're conflicted, not dead against.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer A. The insight in one sentence: when a passage describes 'a growing minority' moving in a new direction and lists specific restrictive proposals, the trend is toward that direction — even if it isn't yet the norm.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'British laissez-faire' framing as evidence of continuing public apathy.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage describes active discussion — Cats Protection's curfew advice, Pirie's buffer zones, growing indoor-only practice. Apathy is the opposite of what's being described.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the curfew and indoor-only mentions and infer most owners are worried.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the passage says 'a growing MINORITY' are restricting their cats — explicitly not most. The trend exists but hasn't reached majority yet.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on outdoor-cat lovers is that they'll resist any restriction outright.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 4 shows that even Cats Protection (a cat-welfare body) recommends a dusk-till-dawn curfew. Cat lovers are conflicted and proposing compromises — not dead against restriction.

var-2026-verb2-ELF-039.technique: On 'what are we told about the debate' questions, separate the current state (most cats outdoor) from the direction of change (growing minority indoor). The 'trend' answer always describes the direction of motion, not the static majority.
var-2026-verb2-ELF-039.pitfall: The cure for picking 'apathy' or 'dead against restriction' answers: count the named experts and proposals. Active debate with curfew recommendations and buffer-zone ideas = engagement, not indifference. Compromise proposals = conflicted, not dead against.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage says 'a growing minority' plus names restrictive proposals (curfew, buffer zones, indoor-only), pick the option that describes a trend toward less outdoor freedom.

var-2026-verb2-ELF-040.solution_path: The closing contrasts British voices (Cummings recommending curfews, Pirie suggesting buffer zones, both seeking compromise) with American Peter Marra: 'I don't think there should be a compromise… It's about people taking responsibility for their animal.' The passage ends 'What that means, however, might prove to be a cultural difference.' Clear cross-Atlantic attitude gap. Answer D.

steps.title: What does the question ask?
steps.text: We need the best summary of the closing remarks specifically — the verdict the passage leaves on the British-vs-American framing of the cat issue.

steps.title: Anchor key vocabulary
steps.text: 'Clear differences' = distinct, identifiable contrasts. 'Cultural difference' = a difference rooted in shared social attitudes, not just policy. When the closing literally says 'cultural difference', look for the option that names the difference.

steps.title: Map the two closing camps
steps.text: British voices: Cummings (dusk-till-dawn curfew, 'sensible compromise'), Pirie (buffer zones, listen to people's concerns). American voice: Marra ('I don't think there should be a compromise… It's about people taking responsibility'). Compromise vs no-compromise — that's the explicit contrast.

steps.title: Read the final line
steps.text: 'But from the perspective of Peter Marra… "I don't think there should be a compromise," he says. "It's about people taking responsibility for their animal." What that means, however, might prove to be a cultural difference.' The passage's last sentence names the difference.

steps.title: Match to an option
steps.text: Option D — 'Clear differences are to be found between British and American attitudes' — is the direct paraphrase of 'cultural difference'. The British compromise stance vs the American no-compromise stance maps onto 'clear differences in attitudes'.

steps.title: Verify against alternatives
steps.text: A says 'profound disagreement on environmental harm' — both sides agree cats hunt wildlife; they disagree on the response. B says British are more ignorant — passage describes informed British debate. C says broad consensus on outdoor access — passage shows opposite: US wants indoor, UK wants compromise.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: Answer D. The insight in one sentence: when a passage closes with explicit comparative phrasing ('a cultural difference'), the right answer paraphrases that phrase — clear differences in attitudes, not in facts.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the disagreement on what-to-do as disagreement on the underlying environmental harm.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows both Marra and the British experts accept cats hunt wildlife. The disagreement is about the RESPONSE (compromise vs no-compromise), not about whether harm is real.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the British not-particularly-concerned framing and infer broader British ignorance.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the closing British voices are well-informed (Cummings of Cats Protection, Pirie the cat ecologist). The British view is engaged, not ignorant — it's just calibrated differently.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a cross-country cat piece is to look for shared belief in outdoor access.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 6 shows the data refutes consensus: US 70% indoor vs UK 70% outdoor. And Marra explicitly rejects compromise. There is no broad consensus on outdoor access in the closing.

var-2026-verb2-ELF-040.technique: On 'how can the closing remarks best be summarised' questions, find the explicit summarising phrase at the very end — 'cultural difference', 'a question of priorities', 'unresolved tension'. The right answer paraphrases that exact phrase.
var-2026-verb2-ELF-040.pitfall: The cure for inventing 'consensus' or 'profound disagreement on facts': separate fact agreement from response disagreement. Both sides agree cats hunt; they differ on what to do. That's a values/attitude split — a 'cultural difference', not a factual one.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the passage ends with the literal phrase 'cultural difference' (or its near-equivalent), pick the option that names attitude differences between the named countries — not consensus or factual disagreement.
